Final Project: Language Models With Different Tokenisations¶

In this project we compared several small GPT-style language models trained on the same long-form book corpus. We started from dataset preparation and tokenisation, then studied compact hyperparameter variations, selected final configurations, trained the final models for longer runs, and evaluated the saved best-validation checkpoints.

We intentionally kept the final comparison focused on the compact parameter studies and the longer 100,000-iteration final runs. The final model choice was based on validation behaviour, perplexity, generalisation gap, model size, and generated samples.

1. Project Goal¶

The goal is to compare small GPT-style language models trained on the same book corpus but using different tokenisation strategies:

  • character-level tokens
  • syllable-level tokens
  • GPT-2/tiktoken word-subword tokens
  • compact local-word tokens

The final comparison focuses on validation loss, validation perplexity, generalisation gap, generated samples, and the practical tradeoff between vocabulary size, model size, and text quality.

Colab Setup: Mount Drive And Extract Computed Models¶

We stored the trained model repository in Google Drive as MyDrive/LM_Project/final_model_repository.zip. This cell mounts Drive, extracts the archive, and sets MODEL_REPOSITORY_DIR for the final evaluation.

In [1]:
# Mount Drive and extract the already-trained model repository and parameter-study CSVs.

from pathlib import Path
import zipfile
from google.colab import drive

drive.mount('/content/drive')

project_drive_dir = Path('/content/drive/MyDrive/LM_Project')
model_zip_path = project_drive_dir / 'final_model_repository.zip'
parameter_zip_path = project_drive_dir / 'parameter_study_artifacts.zip'

MODEL_REPOSITORY_DIR = Path('/content/final_model_repository')
PARAMETER_STUDY_DIR = Path('/content/parameter_study_artifacts')

if not (MODEL_REPOSITORY_DIR / 'manifest.json').exists():
    MODEL_REPOSITORY_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    with zipfile.ZipFile(model_zip_path, 'r') as zf:
        zf.extractall(MODEL_REPOSITORY_DIR)

if not (PARAMETER_STUDY_DIR / 'pg19_parameter_study_manifest.csv').exists():
    PARAMETER_STUDY_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    with zipfile.ZipFile(parameter_zip_path, 'r') as zf:
        zf.extractall(PARAMETER_STUDY_DIR)

print('Using model repository:', MODEL_REPOSITORY_DIR)
print('Using parameter-study artifacts:', PARAMETER_STUDY_DIR)
print('Best checkpoints:')
for path in sorted((MODEL_REPOSITORY_DIR / 'checkpoints').glob('*_best_gpt.pt')):
    print(' -', path.name)

Mounted at /content/drive
Using model repository: /content/final_model_repository
Using parameter-study artifacts: /content/parameter_study_artifacts
Best checkpoints:
 - characters_best_gpt.pt
 - local_words_best_gpt.pt
 - syllables_best_gpt.pt
 - word_subwords_best_gpt.pt

2. Dataset And Preprocessing¶

We loaded a PG-19 sample, inspected basic corpus statistics, removed clearly non-narrative titles using the available title metadata, and built the cleaned corpora used by the tokenisers.

Libraries Installation And Imports¶

We use PyTorch for the GPT model, NLTK for syllable tokenization, and tiktoken for word/subword tokenization.

In [2]:
# Import the main libraries and prepare the working environment.

from pathlib import Path
from itertools import product
import datetime
import gc
import json
import math
import random
import re
import time
import warnings

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import requests
from tqdm.auto import tqdm
from datasets import load_dataset

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.nn import functional as F

from nltk.tokenize import SyllableTokenizer
import tiktoken

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

RANDOM_SEED = 42
random.seed(RANDOM_SEED)
np.random.seed(RANDOM_SEED)
torch.manual_seed(RANDOM_SEED)

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", context="notebook")
plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (10, 5)


device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
print("Device:", device)

PROJECT_DIR = Path.cwd()
CACHE_DIR = PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "cache"
CACHE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

Device: cuda
In [3]:
# Set up output folders for Colab or local execution.
# In Colab we save intermediate artifacts in Drive; locally we save them next to the project.
import os

try:
    import google.colab
    IN_COLAB = True
except ImportError:
    IN_COLAB = False

if IN_COLAB:
    from google.colab import drive
    drive.mount('/content/drive')
    DRIVE_DIR = Path('/content/drive/MyDrive/LM_Project')
    DRIVE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    MODEL_DIR = DRIVE_DIR / 'models'
    ARTIFACT_DIR = DRIVE_DIR / 'artifacts'
    print(f'Colab detected — project files will persist to: {DRIVE_DIR}')
else:
    MODEL_DIR = PROJECT_DIR / 'models'
    ARTIFACT_DIR = PROJECT_DIR / 'artifacts'
    print(f'Local environment — project files will be saved to: {PROJECT_DIR}')

MODEL_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
ARTIFACT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

Drive already mounted at /content/drive; to attempt to forcibly remount, call drive.mount("/content/drive", force_remount=True).
Colab detected — project files will persist to: /content/drive/MyDrive/LM_Project

Dataset Overview¶

We used a PG-19 / Project Gutenberg-style dataset: a language-modeling benchmark made from books published before 1919. PG-19 was useful here because it contains real, long-form prose rather than short fragmented texts. Earlier experiments with poetry and song-lyrics data produced weaker semantic continuity, so we moved to books for the final project.

This helped the models produce more coherent samples than the shorter fragmented-text experiments. The tradeoff was size: PG-19 is much larger, so we streamed a configurable number of books and cached the local sample.

The first corpus kept light book metadata such as title and publication date. The compact local-word model used the text-only corpus so generation stayed in prose form instead of drifting into metadata fields.

2.1 Dataset Loading¶

PG-19 is large, so we did not download the whole dataset by default. Instead, we streamed a configurable number of books, kept the first N_BOOKS records from the training split, and cached them locally as JSONL.

We used a higher N_BOOKS value for the final run and lower values during debugging or CPU tests. When one Hugging Face mirror was unavailable, the loader tried the next candidate.

In [4]:
DATASET_CANDIDATES = [
    "emozilla/pg19",
    "Tanushreeeeee/pg19",
]
DATASET_NAME = None
N_BOOKS = 400
FORCE_RELOAD = False
SAMPLE_CACHE_PATH = CACHE_DIR / f"pg19_book_sample_{N_BOOKS}.jsonl"


def normalize_record(record):
    """
    take one raw dataset record and return a clean, standardized dictionary
    """
    return {
        "short_book_title": str(record.get("short_book_title") or record.get("title") or "Untitled"),
        "publication_date": record.get("publication_date", None),
        "url": str(record.get("url") or ""),
        "text": str(record.get("text") or ""),
    }


if SAMPLE_CACHE_PATH.exists() and not FORCE_RELOAD:
    raw_df = pd.read_json(SAMPLE_CACHE_PATH, lines=True)
    print(f"Loaded {len(raw_df):,} books from cache: {SAMPLE_CACHE_PATH}")
else:
    records = []
    load_error_messages = []
    for candidate_name in DATASET_CANDIDATES:
        try:
            # load the training split using Hugging Face load_dataset
            streamed_dataset = load_dataset(candidate_name, split="train", streaming=True)
            DATASET_NAME = candidate_name
            for record in streamed_dataset:
                normalized = normalize_record(record)
                if normalized["text"].strip():
                    records.append(normalized)
                # stop once we have N_BOOKS (120)
                if len(records) >= N_BOOKS:
                    break
            if records:
                break
        except Exception as exc:
            load_error_messages.append(f"{candidate_name}: {exc}")

    if not records:
        raise RuntimeError("Could not load a PG-19 dataset candidate. Errors:\n" + "\n".join(load_error_messages))

    # Convert the list of book dictionaries into a pandas DataFrame
    raw_df = pd.DataFrame(records)

    # Save the sampled books to the cache file
    # orient = "records" : each row becomes a JSON object
    # lines  = True      : saves as JSONL, one record per line
    # force_ascii = False: keeps non ASCII char instead of escaping them
    raw_df.to_json(SAMPLE_CACHE_PATH, orient="records", lines=True, force_ascii=False)
    print(f"Loaded and cached {len(raw_df):,} books from {DATASET_NAME} at {SAMPLE_CACHE_PATH}")

raw_df.head()

# length statistics for each book
raw_df["raw_char_count"] = raw_df["text"].fillna("").astype(str).str.len()
raw_df["raw_word_count"] = raw_df["text"].fillna("").astype(str).str.split().str.len()
raw_df["raw_paragraph_count"] = raw_df["text"].fillna("").astype(str).apply(lambda value: len([p for p in value.split("\n\n") if p.strip()]))

raw_df[["raw_char_count", "raw_word_count", "raw_paragraph_count"]].describe().T

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 4))

# book char counts
sns.histplot(raw_df["raw_char_count"].clip(upper=raw_df["raw_char_count"].quantile(0.98)), bins=40, ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title("Book character counts")
axes[0].set_xlabel("characters, clipped")

# book word counts
sns.histplot(raw_df["raw_word_count"].clip(upper=raw_df["raw_word_count"].quantile(0.98)), bins=40, ax=axes[1], color="#59A14F")
axes[1].set_title("Book word counts")
axes[1].set_xlabel("words, clipped")

# book paragraph counts
sns.histplot(raw_df["raw_paragraph_count"].clip(upper=raw_df["raw_paragraph_count"].quantile(0.98)), bins=40, ax=axes[2], color="#E15759")
axes[2].set_title("Book paragraph counts")
axes[2].set_xlabel("paragraphs, clipped")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Title-Based Corpus Cleanup¶

The PG-19 records we used do not include a clean genre column, so we could not filter the dataset with a real genre variable. In the first runs we used the fuller corpus, but the generated samples were noticeably worse: the models often mixed narrative prose with biblical language, recipe-like instructions, magazine fragments, poetry, and non-fiction headings.

Because of that, we used an LLM as a support tool to help draft a small title-based exclusion list. The LLM was not used to label every book; it helped us turn the obvious problematic title patterns into keyword groups, such as religious texts, poetry/drama, magazines, history/politics, and manuals. We then applied those terms to the available short_book_title metadata.

This removed 88 clearly non-narrative books from the final sample. The goal was not to make a perfect genre classifier, but to make the training corpus more consistent for prose generation.

In [8]:
# Use the title as a proxy because the dataset does not provide a clean genre column.
# After poor early results on the full corpus, we used an LLM as support
# to turn the most obvious problematic cases into keyword groups.
# The filter does not classify every book: it only removes clearly non-narrative titles.

religious_terms = [
    'bible', 'testament', 'sermon', 'epistle', 'gospel',
    'faith of the millions', 'healing of nations',
]

poetry_and_drama_terms = [
    'adonais', 'divine comedy', 'inferno', 'purgator',
    'poetical works', 'fairies and fusilier', 'vigil of venus',
    'eulogies', 'the trojan women',
]

periodical_and_anthology_terms = [
    'punchinello', 'atlantic monthly', 'mirror of literature',
    'strand magazine', 'great english short-story writers',
    'stories of great american',
]

history_politics_and_culture_terms = [
    'chinese literature', 'moorish literature', 'oriental literature',
    'great events', 'famous historian', 'chronicles of canada',
    'american political ideas', 'plain words from america',
    'mobilizing woman', 'life of hon. william',
]

manuals_and_misc_terms = [
    'household management', 'english housewifery', 'foreign dishes',
    'baseball guide', 'rhetoric and poetry', 'discourses',
    'folk-lore of plants', 'complete works of william shakespeare',
    'works of aphra behn', 'works of charles and mary lamb',
    'towards the goal', 'thaumaturgia', 'klondyke nuggets',
    'the warriors by anna', 'home-life of the lancashire',
]

excluded_title_terms = (
    religious_terms
    + poetry_and_drama_terms
    + periodical_and_anthology_terms
    + history_politics_and_culture_terms
    + manuals_and_misc_terms
)

title_filter_pattern = re.compile(
    '|'.join(re.escape(term) for term in excluded_title_terms),
    re.I,
)

mask = ~raw_df['short_book_title'].str.contains(title_filter_pattern, na=False)
removed_df = raw_df[~mask]
raw_df = raw_df[mask].reset_index(drop=True)

print(f'Title cleanup: kept {len(raw_df)} books, removed {len(removed_df)}')
print('\nRemoved titles:')
for title in sorted(removed_df['short_book_title'].tolist()):
    print(f'  - {title}')

Title cleanup: kept 312 books, removed 88

  - The Mirror of Literature Amusement and Instruction
  - The Mirror of Literature Amusement and Instruction
